Throwing out the baby with the bathwater a little?  I haven't been yet, but some aspects of the current Dynamicland embodiment seem... boldly impractical... if not, date I say... courageous.  :apple:

Having turned it over a bit more, one of my frustrations is that Aspect Oriented Programming didn't become a standard thing because I would like to be able to tell the compiler, "Okay guys, within the important financial calculation package, let's not use doubles, kthx."

Yes, and actually I'd like to chat 1-on-1 because ... well, a selfish reason :slightly_smiling_face: I tried to get involved with WebAssembly but it didn't work out too well because I didn't have any meaningful connections with anybody. So, on the off chance it isn't too late, I am hoping you can help me figure out if it is possible to try again.

Take it with a light heart.  There's a certain irony to printing your program out in order to save it, a bazaar reversal of submitting your 1970s stack if punched cards in order to run a program.  When I see Dynamicland videos, I see a revised history accelerated evolution petri dish at work.  A rough draft, not a polished product, exciting possibilities.  (Anyone here who has read my rants will know find delight when I get to see VR in the same way.)

text: Looking for interesting visual programming interfaces, I’ve been looking at a few games lately that all have programming elements as part of gameplay. Here’s a list of games that I found; if you know of others that fit into that category, please let me know: <https://github.com/stefanlesser/recurse/blob/master/resources/games-about-programming.md>
I would add Factorio to the game list.
I heard about it, but haven’t played it. Can you describe what the programming element of it is?
I'd say the programming is implicit - you have to arrange a series of machines along a 2D grid to perform larger-scale tasks.
(OTOH I hope that the work I do for programmers might become part of the underlying infrastructure of tools for non-programmers.)
(OTOH I hope that the work I do for programmers might become part of the underlying infrastructure of tools for non-programmers.)

Yeah, it's implicit. I haven't played it either, but from what I hear from (coder) friends, you build more and more automated structures with increasing complexity, which revolves around the same space as programming does. I'm not sure if it's "actual" programming or not, but when you start considering visual programming paradigms and whatnot, there's a lot of same stuff there. Compare to something like eg. Minecraft redstone links.
.. and I'd like to take this opportunity to once more assert that I, for one, am pursuing the Future of *Programming*, for *non-Coders* :smile:

Interesting. I see in some of the other examples that you record direct manipulation actions on tables into a script. I’ve been experimenting with that too. How does that work for your users? Do they feel it is more “authentic” to write code like the pros do?

Interesting. I see in some of the other examples that you record direct manipulation actions on tables into a script. I’ve been experimenting with that too. How does that work for your users? Do they feel it is more “authentic” to write code like the pros do?

our users are non programmers, some are sysadmins, some dbas, some managers, they use the manipulation to get the data to fit their needs, that's the part that gets the least complaints and observations (I guess that's a good thing?)

our users are non programmers, some are sysadmins, some dbas, some managers, they use the manipulation to get the data to fit their needs, that's the part that gets the least complaints and observations (I guess that's a good thing?)

the parts that they ask more about that are related to programming are asset management (how do I upload an image and get a reference to it) and what we call "dictionaries" (mappings from keys to values that they can use across visualizations and dashboards and can change in one place to reflect in all places. They care a lot about a thing being the same color (and a specific color) across all dashboards.

the parts that they ask more about that are related to programming are asset management (how do I upload an image and get a reference to it) and what we call "dictionaries" (mappings from keys to values that they can use across visualizations and dashboards and can change in one place to reflect in all places. They care a lot about a thing being the same color (and a specific color) across all dashboards.

After that they care a lot about adding interactivity (if the user clicks this, show that, filter this other thing, open another dashboard) this also brings the need to "override" configs (if user clicks a table row, open that dashboard but filter by this field in the row)

After that they care a lot about adding interactivity (if the user clicks this, show that, filter this other thing, open another dashboard) this also brings the need to "override" configs (if user clicks a table row, open that dashboard but filter by this field in the row)

a long way to say that they care a lot about "configuration management" we provide a "chain" of dictionaries and lookup, there are dictionaries at the user level, then per dashboard, then per visualization, if you lookup key k1 in dict d1, it will look first in the visualization, then the dashboard, then the user, so you can start global and then specialize if needed per dashboard or visualization

a long way to say that they care a lot about "configuration management" we provide a "chain" of dictionaries and lookup, there are dictionaries at the user level, then per dashboard, then per visualization, if you lookup key k1 in dict d1, it will look first in the visualization, then the dashboard, then the user, so you can start global and then specialize if needed per dashboard or visualization
they also care about adding lightweight interactivity, react to clicks to filter, overlay info or open other dashboards and visualizations
they also care about adding lightweight interactivity, react to clicks to filter, overlay info or open other dashboards and visualizations

the programming part, the hardest is to undestand the iterator abstraction we use, I always go back and forth between "iterate by default a single item requrires explicit setup" and "single item by default, iteration requires explicit setup" (the one I'm using now)

the programming part, the hardest is to undestand the iterator abstraction we use, I always go back and forth between "iterate by default a single item requrires explicit setup" and "single item by default, iteration requires explicit setup" (the one I'm using now)
one customer ask a lot about data provenance "show me how this data arrived here"
one customer ask a lot about data provenance "show me how this data arrived here"

one pattern I've seen a lot is that they use the clone feature a lot, each customer has many dashboards that look similar, because when they see one they like or it looks like what they need, they clone it and change it for their needs. I guess that's also "programming by example" :slightly_smiling_face:

one pattern I've seen a lot is that they use the clone feature a lot, each customer has many dashboards that look similar, because when they see one they like or it looks like what they need, they clone it and change it for their needs. I guess that's also "programming by example" :slightly_smiling_face:

regarding "Do they feel it is more “authentic” to write code like the pros do?", I've never had a comment like that, maybe because it doesn't look like coding, so they don't expect it to feel closer to coding, manipulating data a small part of the tasks they do to share a dashboard, so maybe they see it as some advanced configuration thing? I may ask next week when I go to visit some of them

Do you see your competition as Jupyter/R notebooks or Tableau/BI tools?

every comparison is with BI tools, mostly Tableau and PowerBI, our customers haven't seen a lot of notebooks yet, we even introduce them on the big data side when they ask for "data science"
every comparison is with BI tools, mostly Tableau and PowerBI, our customers haven't seen a lot of notebooks yet, we even introduce them on the big data side when they ask for "data science"

I guess it's easy to compare to BI tools because the results are "dashboards", we get resistance from the BI teams because they think we want to take their responsibilities or replace existing tools, we always end up fitting in the sysadmin side (I guess they are more pragmatic) or supported by management complaining about price, how long it takes to get a dashboard or the bottleneck on the BI team

the sharing and permission side seems to be appealing to them, we don't charge per "seat" or user, dashboards can be shared in many ways (public, public but password protected, only to authenticated users, or to specific users and/or groups), I'm not too familiar with the sharing aspects of other tools, but that seems to be a problem for them. They also push a lot for "curation", to share a list of dashboards for a particular user, use case or problem. We are slowly becoming a social network :stuck_out_tongue:

attachments.title: Direct Programming
attachments.text:
To make programming more accessible to regular people it may help to make it less abstract and more concrete. Towards that end I am taking another run at the old ideas of macro recording and Programming by Example. Many such efforts abstracted user actions into conventional code. Instead I co-design the PL and UI to be analogous, so that programs and users have much the same capabilities, and programs look much like recorded transcripts of user actions. Conscientiously following this design principle leads in the opposite direction of current PL trends, towards a deeply imperative semantics and a de-emphasis of functional abstraction.

Any attempt to make programming more concrete faces two challenges: loops and conditionals, for they necessarily deal with hypothetical situations. My approach is to extend the direct manipulation metaphor onto _template_ states that prototype iteration, and _example_ states that witness alternative cases.

it was not meant as a critique, just a comparison to have some sort of discussion, I've heard research people say that papers are a long term discussion between different researchers, since I prefer demos (and it seems you too), I thought that a reply trying to show a different approach under the same structure and topics would be interesting, still thinking on how to structure long term discussions between projects of this sort, I like <@U5TCAFTD3> idea of having a fixed set of problems to solve with different tools and see what works, what doesn't, what's easy and what's hard, but my tool being a stream processing/aggregation thing is not well suited to implement a todo list :slightly_smiling_face:

it was not meant as a critique, just a comparison to have some sort of discussion, I've heard research people say that papers are a long term discussion between different researchers, since I prefer demos (and it seems you too), I thought that a reply trying to show a different approach under the same structure and topics would be interesting, still thinking on how to structure long term discussions between projects of this sort, I like <@U5TCAFTD3> idea of having a fixed set of problems to solve with different tools and see what works, what doesn't, what's easy and what's hard, but my tool being a stream processing/aggregation thing is not well suited to implement a todo list :slightly_smiling_face:

the main difference is kind of fundamental, your tool understand types, so a collection has a type for the items, then you can show a "schema" of the item to operate on it as an explicit way to say "over all values of this shape", in my case since the inputs are schemaless the most I can do is infer the schema but I haven't done that yet, that's why I have the explicit iterator value, which is better than explicit imperative loops but next time I may go with "iteration by default, single item is a special case", something I've been exploring a little with the idea of an path item that represents a "column", that is, all items with that key in a colection, the column explicitly applies to all items and if used in a value context will work as a generator for that column (will generate a list of values for that field in the collection)
